audit_items.proof_trace: sass_value_get_tag() itself (sass_values.cpp:17) makes zero allocations — it is a trivial getter. However, in c2ast() (the function calling it), SASS_LIST and SASS_MAP cases allocate via raw SASS_MEMORY_NEW into unguarded raw pointers (List* l, Map* m). If any recursive c2ast() call throws — triggered when a list/map element has tag SASS_ERROR or SASS_WARNING (which calls error() unconditionally) — the containing List* or Map* object is leaked because no try/catch or RAII wrapper guards the allocation. Smart pointer aliases List_Obj/Map_Obj exist but are not used at these sites.
audit_items.attack_scenario: A custom C function registered via the libsass plugin API returns a SASS_LIST or SASS_MAP value containing a nested SASS_ERROR element; the top-level check in eval.cpp:1103-1112 passes because the outer tag is SASS_LIST, c2ast() allocates the List container, then throws on the nested error element, leaking the container and any already-appended child nodes each call.
